raw:
{
  "weights": {
    "R1.1": 0.7,
    "R1.2": 0.7,
    "R1.3": 0.7,
    "R1.4": 0.7,
    "R1.5": 0.0,
    "R1.6": 0.3,
    "R1.7": 0.3,
    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": ["R1.1", "R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.4", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"],
  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "Any response to 'What do you think?' requires reconstructing the thesis (adaptive per-query routing for climate emulation) and evaluating the contribution claim before anything else can be assessed.",
    "R1.2": "The artifact contains a notable inferential leap in the non-stationarity argument — asserting that routing thresholds calibrated on 2014–2017 remain valid under SSP5-8.5 projections because router inputs co-shift with emulator inputs. This syllogism warrants logical scrutiny regardless of whether the user wants scientific or rhetorical feedback.",
    "R1.3": "Headline quantitative claims (63% compute reduction, 4% CRPS penalty, router trained on 8 years of paired forecasts) are prominent and must be traced to experimental evidence; without knowing the full paper this is a primary axis of concern.",
    "R1.4": "The artifact explicitly asserts 'first work to apply learned per-query routing to climate emulation' and 'substantially advances the state of the art' — both direct novelty claims that activate assessment of the delta versus nearest prior work (e.g., mixture-of-experts climate models, static lead-time partitioning systems)."
  },
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "Should the response prioritize evaluating the scientific and logical validity of the claims (R1.2/R1.3 load-bearing — e.g., is the non-stationarity argument sound, are the numbers credible), or evaluating the novelty and positioning relative to prior work (R1.4 load-bearing — e.g., does the 'first work' claim hold against adaptive-compute or mixture-of-experts literature)?"
}

rationale.R1.1: Any response to 'What do you think?' requires reconstructing the thesis (adaptive per-query routing for climate emulation) and evaluating the contribution claim before anything else can be assessed.
rationale.R1.2: The artifact contains a notable inferential leap in the non-stationarity argument — asserting that routing thresholds calibrated on 2014–2017 remain valid under SSP5-8.5 projections because router inputs co-shift with emulator inputs. This syllogism warrants logical scrutiny regardless of whether the user wants scientific or rhetorical feedback.
rationale.R1.3: Headline quantitative claims (63% compute reduction, 4% CRPS penalty, router trained on 8 years of paired forecasts) are prominent and must be traced to experimental evidence; without knowing the full paper this is a primary axis of concern.
rationale.R1.4: The artifact explicitly asserts 'first work to apply learned per-query routing to climate emulation' and 'substantially advances the state of the art' — both direct novelty claims that activate assessment of the delta versus nearest prior work (e.g., mixture-of-experts climate models, static lead-time partitioning systems).

parsed.clarification_question: Should the response prioritize evaluating the scientific and logical validity of the claims (R1.2/R1.3 load-bearing — e.g., is the non-stationarity argument sound, are the numbers credible), or evaluating the novelty and positioning relative to prior work (R1.4 load-bearing — e.g., does the 'first work' claim hold against adaptive-compute or mixture-of-experts literature)?
